narrative.preview: The very first double round-robin tournament of the League! Teams in each Division were randomly selected, with 6 in Premier and the remaining 4 in Qualifier. Wasn't sure what to expect, though as I'm sure you've guessed, it was riveting.

teams.writeup: Closed out the season on a 3-1 run to finish 3-7, taking down #1 seeded Fire Drilled in the regular season finale. Darwin's Doom ranked #1 in fewest turns needed in wins (6.33) on average. True to identity, they were quick out the gate and opponents struggled to damage them early on. Hence their league-leading Win-Life differential (23.67). In longer games, Darwin was man-handled. In their 7 losses, Darwin had the third worst point differential in the league at -22.29. In the playoffs, their first round adversary was NLFY, which was perfect considering NLFYs weakness against quicker teams (7 tpg in losses). Both teams experienced anomalous draws, yet Darwin couldn't capitalize, falling to the higher seed in 10 turns. Irrespective of outcome in the playoffs, the 3-1 close gives Darwin great momentum entering Season 2.

Hard to find the silver lining for the Slivers. Season 1 saw a total collapse, going 0-5 in the second half of the season despite a promising 2-3 start (close loss to #1 Fire Drilled and victory over #2 NLFY). The lackluster finish has them dead last in the standings, which means certain demotion to the Qualifier bracket in Season 2. In losses, the Slivers were at least knocking opponent's life totals by about a third, which led the division. The Slivers met up with #1-seed F-Drilled in first round of the playoffs and played them well, dropping FDs point total to 11 by the end of the game. It'll be interesting to see how the Slivers do in Season 2 against lesser competition in the Qualifier.

The Commissioner was particularly worried about this team. During the pre-season, B&B was wiping the floor with opponents. No one could cast a thing; B&B countered anything that was summoned, or copied/stole any opponent creature fortunate to make it onto the battlefield. This team was brutal. This season, though, their promising 3-1 start looks a bit misleading, with only one of those wins coming against a playoff team. B&B struggled mightily from that point on, dropping the next 5 matches by an average 20-points per game. B&B is setup to win long, drag'em out sluggers, which showed through in their average turns per won games (11.25 avg.) - a mark 33% longer than the league average. By playoff time, B&B ran into familiar foe in Smashed Potatoes in the first round and was ended in just 7-turns despite having the early momentum.

A prime example of a team that wins the games you should win, and loses the games it shouldn't. Swept the basement, mopped up by the top dogs. The key were two wins against B&B, which propelled Smashed Potatoes to a #3-seed lock. Smashed Potatoes usually finds itself in a 10-turn game irrespective of outcome. Smashed Potatoes opened the playoffs with B&B, who it beat twice in the regular season by an average of 24 points per game. In the first round, Smashed Potatoes slapped them by a whopping 33 points - the second-highest mark of the season League-wide - then dropped to NLFY in Round 2.

A simple blueprint, age old favorites and a few new combos landed FD the #1 seed after finishing 8-2. Not necessarily the most well-funded group, but F-Drilled made the most of its parts and showed that early on, staying perfect thru 4 Rounds. In fact, they were one mana shortage away from being perfect thru 9. FD can play short or long, winning twice in 5 turns, yet clocking a victory after 15 turns in Week 7. Fire Drilled opened the playoffs with Sliver Lining, enjoyed a bye in the second round, then ran out of land-to-stand on in the final against NLFY.

Arguably the most well-constructed Team heading into the regular season. Armed with the second fewest number of attacking creatures, NLFY was set to win by resource depletion and that scheme played out well. There are as many mana kill cards as there are creatures in this deck. An 0-2 start was followed by a 6-game winning streak by an average of over 20 ppg. Black Vise won MVP 3 times over that stretch. NLFY took both regular season match-ups against Darwin's Doom, and once more in the first round of the playoffs. NLFY then bested Smashed Potatoes by 12 in Round 2, and sealed the crown with a smooth victory in the finals. All hail the victors!!

Entered Season 1 with strong support from the Commissioner. Despite constant revisions to core strategy and makeup, Extinction still failed to break free from the shackles of the Qualifier Division, going 3-3. Extinction prefers a quicker pace, using only 9.51 turns per game in wins vs. 11.67 in losses. They sport the lowest differential in the league (13.67) and yet owned the largest point differential in losses. In other words, Extinction is quite mortal, getting hit reasonably hard in wins, and then completely slapped in losses. At least in the losses, Extinction holds on far longer than most, forcing opponents to an average of 11.67 turns in a loss.

Truth be known, thrulls were the focal point of the first deck ever created by your League Commissioner. Seeing the now-20yr old vision complete was pretty enthrulling. Are they competitive? Nope, not really. Thrull Frottle went 2-4 in Season 1. There are a few positive signs - the Thrull's found ways to damage opponents in losses more effectively than most. The Thrull's prefer a longer game (11 tpg in a win) than shorter (8.54 tpg in a loss). The return of Hecatomb directly resulted in the Thrulls' final win of the season.

The most well-funded Team in Season 1! The forever-noteworthy additions of Illusionary Mask and Phyrexian Dreadnought had the Commissioner more excited for Dead Heads than any other Team. This season, the Heads led the division in point differential during wins (20.67), and also damaged their opponents more than anyone else when they lost, knocking them down 12.77 points on average, more than doubling the league average (5.63). Dead Heads delivered only slightly, going 3-3 to close out Season 1 and earn a trip north to the Premier Division in Season 2.

Biggest surprise of the Qualifier Division! Team 10 slipping by the competition with a 4-2 record and, yet, the lowest Win-Point differential (13.50). Team 10 prefers a longer game with wins taking a division-highest 11.25 turns per game. One of its two losses came at the hands of Extinction in a blowout despite going 11-turns - right in Team 10s wheelhouse. In match-ups against Dead Heads, Team 10 won by an average of only 8-points with both games coming down the wire, barely standing with only 2.5 points remaining in those wins.

narrative.preview: Newcomer Team 10 nearly snags the #1-seed. A Season 1-stalwart turns into a punching bag. All but one team fighting for their playoff lives entering Week 12. 'Ups and downs' don't quite do this one justice.

teams.writeup: 3-2 in Weeks 1-6. Continued to go 3-4 the rest of the way after some mid-season changes. They lost Game 1 of the first round against Smashed Potatoes, then took the final two games before getting smoked in the semi-final by Fire Drilled 2-0. An up and down season for Team 10. They got lucky early, then saw a dramatic shift in strategy, and turned over most of their roster as they searched for a core identity. By seasons end, Team 10 had undergone such radical change from their initial hodgepodge roster that a full rebrand was in play.

A .500 record in Season 1 followed by a game over .500 in Season 2. Extinction is like the Atlanta Hawks (between 2009-2013) of the League - they're good, in contention for a playoff spot but not a championship. That said, I'm not sure what they are yet. For now, a few of core pieces (Wrath of God, Llurgoyf) will be repurposed for Swamp Things while the remainder of the roster watches eagerly from the sidelines. Goodnight for now, Extinction.

Have to admit, I loved this Team. It was the emergence of Illusionary Mask-Dreadnought combo, with some graveyard resurrection, and then the Goblins running amok. Beautiful. Did it make a lick of sense, no. Let's just say it was hastily assembled, and now each of those three parts is central to a separate deck. The Heads completed a short stint at 7-11 over two seasons. Thanks for coming out, Heads.

Another melting pot of old cards thrown together with no actual goal. This was a fun ride for a season but there's no future for this team. The record is misleading, there's no central theme, yet there are some valuable spare parts that will be used in other decks. Somehow Dance of Many made it out every single game, and I hate Dance of Many.

The strangest of the to-be-disassembled bunch. By the sixth or seventh turn in every game, it always looked like B&B had no chance of winning. I waited for the game changing card to arrive each turn, and it just never came. A closer look revealed a few flaws, like holding everyone's mana hostage with Stasis while also trying to mill the opponents' library, which requires mana! Nothing about that makes sense. They're 10-12 over two seasons, and seem stuck. Alas, time to tear things down.

EVERYONE had lands in Season 2!! Well, not everyone, but the four teams who were responsible for all 7 of NLFYs losses certainly did. Two common themes in those defeats; (a) 3 of the 4 teams play with black as one of their colors and (b) they had no ability to remove a card from the battlefield. NLFY enters each game with a very thin margin for error by not stacking more removal cards in their deck. They're 12-10 in two seasons, but are only one of two teams with a crown. Roster turnover most likely isn't the answer, but maybe a few sideboard cards are when facing other teams with black in their color schemes.

Are they going small? Big? Both? Neither? Core to Smashed Potatoes's strategy is land destruction, and yet there are creatures on the current roster who need seven-mana, and even nine-mana, to be summoned. How does that work...at all? A 12-10 record isn't bad, but it isn't great either. Nothing worse than being stuck in the middle. Three of Smashed Potatoess 6 regular season wins were due to land destruction. Most of their losses were due to too few creatures on the battlefield.

I'm not sure what to make of this team yet. They've been destroyed in two seasons, going 6-16. When they have the right mana-creature combination they can be devastating, but without it, they sputter. A few tweaks could go a long way here, but I have a feeling it'll take some time to find the right combination. I'm hell-bent on keeping this a creature-heavy outfit so we'll start there.

Season 2 saw a slower Darwin Team. They needed 4 more turns on average to win vs the first season, but sealed a better record and higher playoff seed as a result. Darwin was in the middle of the fray nearing the end of the season and could have dropped out of the playoffs entirely had other matches gone differently. Come playoff time, Darwin drew B&B after their first-round bye. In the playoffs, Darwin made short work of B&B in two games winning by an average of 20 points. Darwin's losses highlighted the lack of heft in their creature base, so additional force may be needed.

What a turnaround, going 5-1 in the second half of the regular season. They beat everyone in the Qualifier at least once and really started to firm up their identity as a creature sacrificing machine. In fact, the Thrulls were one loss away from ascending into the Premier Division. A closer look at the Frottle wins sees three of them coming as a result of low mana or creature output from their opponents. Still, the Thrulls should be a tougher out entering the third season. Well done by the Thrulls.

Two first place finishes in a row for the now-defending champ. They started out slow, then reeled off 7 of their next 8 games before sweeping the playoffs. They were not challenged in the playoffs, roasting opponents in a blitzing 6-turns per game. They even won in 5-turns twice to sweep Team 10. Darwin pushed them to the brink in the playoff finale with FD outlasting Darwin 4 to -5 after 13 turns. Fire Drilled is now 16-6 over two seasons, and looks far and away the most formidable team in The League.

narrative.preview: Two highly anticipated entrants into the league (El Kamino & The Doldrums) turn heads. Sliver Lining rises up from a 6-16 all-time record to take the #1-seed. And the likeliest of unlikely Teams takes home the crown. The traditional two division format was ditched for one bruising mosh pit of 10 teams in a double-round robin, and the carnage did not disappoint.

teams.writeup: 'El Kamino is broken.' This was the inserted comment after their fifth game of the season - a 36-point drubbing by Smashed Potatoes. SOMEHOW, this group took on too much at once. They had some strong wins - NLFY by 20, Darwin by 19, & the Boss twice by an avg. of 23 - but they had one of the worst loss differentials in the league (>22ppg). Mana issues were present too often for this tri-color squad, as they struggled to summon what they needed when they needed it. Safe the Commissioner isn't ready for that level of complexity in deck building. So long, Clones.

I know, I know. 'How is this team not in the Deconstructed group after going 2-16?' Well, there are three reasons: (a) they were being played incorrectly for most of the season, (b) white wasn't a helpful partner-color, and (c) they have help on the way. Up until Week 16, The Doldrums strategy was to mill-first, counter-second and kill creatures as needed. That week, however, the Doldrums flipped the script and secured their first victory over the then-desperate El Kamino, winning on the 16th turn despite being down 19 to 1. It was clear at that point to counter-first, mill-second. With changes in store for the lowly Doldrums, the League finally gets their first mono-blue deck. Bazinga!

The Swamp Things feel like a ball of clay right now. It's hard to know what to make of them. Over the course of their inaugural season, they dropped a color (white), swapped out ten more cards, and still made the playoffs at an even 9-9. Their win total is a bit deceiving, as only 4 of them look strong at this point. The Things' graveyard resurrection strategy is coming along, and yet it's not clear if the inclusion of green helps this Team at all. Some fans are wondering whether green should be swapped for blue to give the Things a bit more protection.

End of last season had them as an Up'n'Comer. This time around, it feels like they've regressed. The Frottlers had two good wins in the regular against playoff teams, two anomalous wins, then four against the bottom-dwellers. A total of five losses were due to mana issues (led the league). Something's not right here, and the Commissioner has a radical thought as to why; Thrulls stink!! They're terrible! An entire deck built around them? Cmon 10-yr-old-me!! A major retooling is in store here because this Team isn't going away. How to make them more competitive is the question, and the answer likely has to do with less Thrulls.

A 2-4 start had them looking at a 7-11 record over their previous 18 games - basically, since they won the championship in Season 1. Fortunately, they turned things around in the final two trimesters of the season, going 9-3 to secure the #2-seed. In Season 3, NLFY won games 33% faster than they did in Season 2. They even took home the Murphy Award for most aggressive team. Sounds lovely, but in four of their wins, opponents only mustered 1 or 2 mana drawn. On the other end, NLFY lost 4-games to lack of defense against artifacts and creatures. In the playoffs, a lack of defense against black creatures specifically plagued them as they were upset by the Swamp Things in Round 1.

Despite a first round playoff exit, this third season marked a sizable improvement for the Smashed Potatoesrs. They secured their highest win percentage, largest point differential in wins (more than doubling last season's), and were beating opponents at a faster pace than ever before. Their point differential led the league this season. The shift toward a smaller, nimbler team worked out extremely well and was critical in securing a top 4-seed. Problem is that they're still getting beat. The answer to 'Can this Team win a Championship?' may still be 'no', especially given the increase in competition throughout the league.

Well, it's happened; the Drillers got knocked around for the first time in three seasons. Their win percentage has gone from 80% to 75% to 61% season over season. They got beat by familiar foe Darwin's Doom in the first round of the playoffs after beating them in the championship last season. The number of turns it took for them to lose was the lowest it's been - meaning other teams are beating them to the punch faster than ever before. On the plus side, they lost four games due to mana issues (an anomaly), and remained competitive in the others losing by only 13.2 points per game (2nd best in the league). There might be a little wiggle room on the Drillers roster to hasten their speed.

Have to admit, the Commissioner did NOT see this type of finish for Darwin. Two straight appearances in the championship round, even if it came with two straight losses. Darwin was a shining 9-5 heading into the final four game stretch. Some of their success could be attributed to significant roster turnover, with 12 players being swapped out during the season to increase their speed, defense and battlefront heft. A Commish favorite in Force of Nature was signed in Week 13. They faltered, going 1-3 late to drop from projected #4-seed to the fifth seed. Their road to the finals wasn't easy; they beat the stalwart Drillers 2-1 in Round 1, exterminated the high-flying Slivers 2-0 in Round 2 thanks to Hurricane, then went down fighting in the finals 2-1 to fellow-green-team Boss Fight. Strong output for Darwin with an even more positive outlook in the season ahead.

Whoaaaaa baby! How 'bout them Slivers! Big, big props to the new addition City of Brass. The previously 6-16 Slivers built off of their new flexibility in the mana department by burying opponents by the largest margin of any team in Season 3, which propelled them to take home their first #1-seed. The Slivers were also the second fastest team in victories, taking only 7.54 turns on average to blast opponents. They rode the in-season momentum into the playoffs, blanked the Thrulls in the first round, and were then upset in the second round by Darwin. Losing to Darwin in 2-games marked the third time this season that the Slivers lost to this squad, the only opponent with such an advantage over the #1-seed. Mana played a part in four of their losses. Great, great season by the Slivers.

Who else?! The Boss lives up to the name in their inaugural season, beating down any and all opposition who dared approach their level, at least in the playoffs. The regular season told a different story as Boss Fight scratched and clawed their way to a playoff berth as the #6-seed with a 9-9 record. When they lost, they lost hard, owning the second largest deficit in losses (22.67 points per game). But did it matter? Nope. They took care of business against #3-seed Smashed Potatoes winning 2 games to 1 with a punishing finale where they won by 29 points. Round two had them paired up with fellow upsetter Swamp Things, winning again two games to one with a shutout in the finale. Then, Darwin. The first game was almost anomalous for the Boss as they took a 12-point victory by the sixth turn. Game 2 went the other way. In the championship finale, a dog fight ensued, concluding with a 23-point T.K.O. ushered by the Boss to fellow green-team Darwin. Now, listen, Boss Fight will NOT be included as a regular team in Season 4. They proved our entire fan base correct by taking home the crown in their inaugural season which, though this may appear controversial, wasn't supposed to happen.

narrative.preview: A hearty 'Welcome back!' to former-underdog Team 10. A bittersweet 'So long' to El Kamino gave way to a new bruiser; Bad Ass Fault. The Doldrums significantly revamp their roster for an equally disastrous season. The Swamp Things find their way. Fire Drilled sputtered into an incomprehensible tailspin, and multiple teams forced into rebrand. Season 4 guaranteed to be more competitive for each team, more fun for each fan and definitely more time consuming for each Commish.

teams.writeup: Annnnd that's all folks! The only team to post an all-time win percentage under 20% is finally, and without further question, being put to rest. SOMEHOW, The Doldrums lulled us to sleep early on this season starting 3-3 and faking us into believing their Season 3 foibles were resolved. No sir. From there, the wheels came off. They finished on a 2-13 slide to end their second season at a paltry 5-15, not to mention a sterling 0-6 record against the top 3 seeds. This squad was doomed upon conception - a team built predominantly on counters meant ideally all defense and next to no offense. They were doling out only 6-7 points of damage per game! Not only that, but this Team finished with the second-worst Universal DPI ever. The one Team they beat? Themselves from Season 3. Yuck.

A begrudging end to a hopelessly hopeful squad. The glimmer of faith this season was in harnessing a secondary strategy centered around enlivening artifacts. Here's a comment after an ugly win in Week 11, 'It feels like the artifact-to-life concept for Extinction isn't real - haven't seen it play out once this season.' Core to the roster was the ability to destroy opposing creatures, yet their most frequent reason for defeat was due to opposing creatures! As a result, X finishes the season with a bottom-6 U-DPI mark all-time; one of the least powerful teams ever. With their hopeful hopes now lost, and a few key parts sold off to the highest bidders, X fans become ex-fans after heading for the exits.

Oh, how the mighty have fallen. Season 4 was not kind to the Drillers, not in the slightest. The former champ started off winless through the first 8 weeks of the season, and didn't nab their second win until Week 13. One of the primary reasons for their demise has been the recent influx of enchantment spells. Most every team has added them, and even altered other areas of their roster to generate a unique blend of offense and defense. FD has no way to get rid of opposing enchantments, or any other non-creature resources. Not only that, but the two cards - Mana Flare and Furnace of Rath - can actually aid the opponent. No wonder the Drillers slid so far and so fast. The League's simply not the same without a contentious Driller Team.

The sadness is real. The Thrulls were initially cast in a spotlight deck 20 years ago, but we've reached their expiration date. For 4 seasons now, they have been the perpetual underdog, tied tightly to the 'thrull' identity and dealing with the consequences (Read: Getting smoked more aggressively than a yule log on Christmas). As the league competition level increased, the Frottle's win percentage decreased -- 50% in Season 2 to 44% in Season 3 to 35% this season. A look at their wins this season tells an interesting story; 4 were due to opposing mana struggles, 1 was against the league's worst team ever, another was a close win against Team 10, and then there's one more from Week 19. But Week 19 is where things got interesting. The Frottle signed 9 new free agents prior to their match-up against the then-2nd place Slivers. The game went 12 long turns as the Thrulls took it to the Slivers, winning by 2 points in a dog fight. Say goodbye to the Frottlers, and hello to The Attritionists.

A beloved squad, Team 10 was an early fan favorite who surpassed expectations in their comeback season going 10-10. They beat up on the bottom of the league, going 5-1, and get pummeled by the elite, losing 5 of 6 against the top 3 teams. Their soft initial schedule pumped them up to even 3rd place by Week 7. After sliding into 9th place by Week 11, the 10 traded for Titania's Song from Extinction to help throw a little spice into their recipe. Team 10 took it to #2-seed Bad Ass Fault in their first game of the playoffs, going down by a final score of 1 to -1. Had they one more turn, Titania's Song would've undoubtedly swung the game. Two games later, their season was ended in a doors-blown-off 43 point loss. A stumbled-into-strategy of marrying Titania's Song with Yawgmoth Demon showed serious promise, and is the very catalyst behind the coming rebrand to the Frozen Stiffs.

This season's winner of the 'So Much Fun to Play With You Can't Believe It' Award marched to a 10-10 record behind their wonderfully unique graveyard-resuscitation scheme. It took some time for the roster to gel, but additions like ultra-heavyweight Polar Kraken significantly amped up their offensive capabilities. Make no mistake, the tag team of Buried Alive & Living Death is the heart and soul of this team - accounting for Game MVP in 6 of their ten victories. Their 1-5 record against the top teams isn't great, sure, but they weren't in full-form yet. No matter, they still had enough oomph to take it at the Slivers in Round 1 of the playoffs, blasting them in 5-turns (30pt win), then 6-turns (26pt win) and finally 8-turns (12pt win). They hung tough against Bad Ass Fault too. High hopes for this squad heading into Season 5.

Stamp it, one more solid season for NLFY. In this one, they push to a 13-7 record, set a personal best in Damage Power Index rating en route to a 4-seed, yet manage to do so operating at the 5th slowest win pace of all time, averaging 13.15 turns per win. Oddly, that win pace is almost 2x what they averaged last year when they went 11-7. No major changes to their roster, yet as radical a change in pace as we've seen. But success in the regular season only gets you so far, as, for the third straight year, No Landia fails to escape the first round of the playoffs - this time losing to the eventual champ; Smashed Potatoes.

Last season's most surprising Team, the Slivers clock a 14-6 record in this one and pull down the #3-seed. The Slivers have quickly become a team no one wants to play - they're the only squad in the league with a 4-turn win, and they operate at the fastest win pace of any team, needing only 7.86 turns to down their opponents. They dealt the most damage per game too - at 20.25 points per game. Did we mention their all-time Top 5 U-DPI rating? Are you getting the point? Sliver Lining had a historic regular season and yet they only managed the 3-seed. As potent as the Slivers were, they faltered mightily down the stretch, losing twice in the last 4 weeks. This changed the entire playoff picture and may have had a major hand in the Slivers' demise. Without those losses, they take home the top seed. One thing is for sure, it's safe to say the Slivers are pissed off, and pumped up about next season.

Anyone see this one coming? Neither did we. The Fault, in their first season, go from Zero to Hero as they make history by winning 12 straight, pushing them from 11th place to a #2-seed. Their U-DPI? The second highest of all-time, behind only an uncontested Fire Drilled team from Season 1. Absolutely stunning season from the Fault. What's working for them? Bad Ass Fault's roster make-up is arguably the most complementary of any team - they do a very nice job blending land destruction with removing cards from the opposing hand. If/When successful, opponents have no resources to do anything, and are left for dead. This Team didn't feel another loss until Round 4 in Game 2 against a solid Swamp Things team. They managed to dispatch the Things four games later, then saw their luck run out as they went down to Smashed Potatoes in the finals 4-2.

The Doom have done it! The illustrious #1-seed is theirs to have. In the most hotly contested regular season in history, the Doom were demonstrative in their success, holding the top seed in 16 of the 22 weeks of the regular season. They showed no significant weaknesses as they swept most of the competition, splitting games with the other two top teams, and losing only one season series (to NLFY). Their roster appeared very well-balanced, as 11 different cards won game MVP during the regular season. They showed more versatility this season in terms of game length, winning five games in 10 or more turns. They, once again, handled long-time rival Fire Drilled in the playoffs winning in 3-straight. Round 2 was quite different - Smashed Potatoes had their way, winning in six games on the backs of land-destruction cards like Armageddon and Cataclysm.

There's only one way to start this off. Let's look at an excerpt from last season's recap on Smashed Potatoes - 'The answer to Can this Team win a Championship? may still be No, especially given the increase in competition throughout the league.' Welp, there's your reverse jinx everyone!! All Hail the Potatoes!! They truly defy the odds by taking home their first championship in their 4th season, silencing the haters in the process. Their regular season was solid, but nothing spectacular, finishing at a game above .500 (11-9) with an 0-6 record against the so-called-top-dogs. Their regular season, and more importantly playoff success came from league-MVP; Armageddon. The Jenny Award winner just decimated opponents time and time again, sometimes multiple times at a time. Smashed Potatoes didn't exactly waltz through the playoffs. Rather, they outlasted opponents, surviving in what looked to be a dog-fight each round; 3-1 vs. NLFY, 4-2 vs. Darwin, 4-2 vs. Bad Ass Fault. This is what they're built for - just when opponents gain a bit of momentum, or have a little light, they slap another game-changing card on to the table. They're scrappy, versatile and have the toughest defense of anyone in the league. This marks the second time a #5-seed has taken home the championship. An extremely impressive march to the crown for Smashed Potatoes.

narrative.preview: Count it, 1-2-3-4-5 new teams joined the fray and were collectively skewered. Bad Ass Fault continued their dominance and took home the #1-seed. Sliver Lining and No Lands For You sharpened their dulling blades. Fire Drilled removed the blindfold. Darwin lost their shirt and the Things got feisty.

teams.writeup: A team who made it to the semi-finals is being dismantled?? It sounds ridiculous, but yes. The Law had a nice season on paper, winning nearly half their games, doling out absurd amounts of damage, and setting a few League records along the way. When they were on, they were deadly. This is precisely how they won their first round series against the Swamp Things - drawing their combo before they could. Their Pandemonium + Dreadnought (Panda-Nought) combination made league history. They now own: #1 in Fastest Victory (3 turns), #1 in Largest Margin of Victory (46 points), #1 in Most Damage Inflicted in a Single Game (60 points), and #1 in Fastest Avg. Turns to Win for a Season (6.13 turns per game). Find the combo fast, win the game. But don't find the combo, or find it too late, lose the game - and those games just weren't fun. Most teams in this league are built to play offense and defense. The Law were an all-offense squad. This ain't a league for ducks.

Odd year for the Frozen Stiffs. They were in 2nd place by Week 6 riding high on the coattails of their staunch defense and Team MVP, Winter Orb. They started 3-0 by knocking off three playoff teams. Then, a free-fall to 10th place by Week 10 where they stayed until Week 17, even losing five in a row at one point. Frozen's regression seemed to center around a main theme; work. There was just too much of it. The roster turned over to place more of an emphasis on Yawgmoth Demon, but 'ol Yawggy's got some serious drawbacks and the Stiffs found themselves breaking their backs to even have a chance each game. Their playoff run featured a 2-games-to-1 victory over one of the worst teams in history (The Attritionists) where they won by an average of 4.5 points, followed by a sweep by Darwin's Doom.

Well, that didn't really work. Didn't work at all, really. There was no leap or jump forward, or even a jog in place. The Atts got their rears handed to them time and again and showed few signs of a congealing strategy. It's unclear how/where to double-down on this roster for them to become competitive. All four of their wins featured a different Game MVP. One of their new entrants is on the way out to start his own team; Underworld Dreams. Usually there's a light at the end of the tunnel, but The Atts might be in a cave - their U-DPI rating is 2nd worst all-time. Much, much help is needed, and maybe a B12 shot.

Nice bounce-back season for the Drillers! They doubled their win total from Season 4, and improved their U-DPI by about threefold. Excellent improvement despite their rosters' revolving door with over 30 player transactions. That's the new high water mark for roster turnover in one season. Once the playoffs rolled around, most of their strategy was refocused on flame-throwing opponents with quick-hitter spells, like Lightning Bolt. The results said it all - they stifled the #4-seed Slivers in a three game sweep and then took the eventual runner-up, #9-seed Darwin, to a Game 7. The new burn strategy was a strong departure from what we've known Fire Drilled to be, hence the pending rebrand to Dark 'N Stormy. The burn strategy will arm the former-great with much more agility in a mana-strapped game.

An encouraging first season! They snagged a few wins against top competition, rumbled into a promising strategy and adapted a nice roster as the season progressed. Their identity started to crystallize as the season concluded, actually, as a souped up defensive squad - the only one aiming to deal damage via opposing mana supply (thanks Manabarbs!). It might be overzealous, but they almost dispatched Darwin's Doom in the first round of the Qualifier Bracket after winning Game 1. Heading into Season 6, the Hot will add key defensive pieces like more Maze of Ith, an Invoke Prejudice and a Wall of Tears. Offense, however, is a different story. The Hot were one of the least potent teams in the league, doling out only 11.45 damage points per game (3rd worst in the league).

The Sliver finally plateaued after owning the League's best record over the prior two seasons (27-11), going 12-8 as they locked up a #4 seed in the playoffs. Season 5 was a tale of two halves, going 5-6 in the 1st half of the season, then 7-2 in the 2nd to climb from 9th place in Week 11 to 4th at the close. Their quickened pace to victory, at 6.58 turns, was good enough for #3 All-Time. The Slivers lost three straight to #5-seed Fire Drilled in the first round. For the second year in a row, Sliver Lining were dealt a bad matchup. The Drillers were able to stand toe-to-toe with the Slivers' absurd creature base, destroying at least five of them per game, with more than enough firepower to march on. Last season's recap seems even more applicable this year: 'One thing is for sure, it's safe to say the Slivers are pissed off, and pumped up about next season.'

Ahhh, the former champ. Best regular season in team history at 14-6 with a 70% win percentage to lock down the #3-seed. Their U-DPI irked up to a sound 0.116, also a team record. Although, the Potatoes may have been this season's unlucky team - 6.83 turns (3rd fastest all-time) was the average amount of time it took for opponents to best them. Nine is the number of different players to take home a game MVP award during the regular season. In eight of their 14 wins, Smashed Potatoes played a game-changing role, and takes home Team MVP for the season as a result. Ironically, they had a few holes in their defense - creatures in the regular season (4 losses) & artifacts in the playoffs (2) where they were bested by longtime foe; No Lands For You.

Now THAT's a season! The Things blew doors off of just about everyone, notching a win against 9 of 10 total teams to go 15-5 in their third season. Their wins were offensive and downright demonstrative, pulverizing opponents by a record-setting 25.50 points per game. Total damage done to opponents this season was a ridiculous 510 - far and away the most in League history by over 100 points. That's truly absurd and speaks to the powerful card combination of Buried Alive & Living Death (Team Co-MVPs). Unsurprisingly, their U-DPI (0.191) is also #1 All-Time. The Things were beasts in Season 5 and very few teams were prepared to handle their graveyard resurrection strategy. Four of their five losses came against mana destruction teams. Their first round series against Murphy's Law exposed their reliance on the Certain Death combo. They simply couldn't draw it enough to win.

Treason! Villainy!! For the first time in the history of the League, we have scandal on our hands! A major rule violation was discovered mid-season that completely nullified a core part of their strategy. Turns out discarding a Rukh Egg from one's hand does not qualify a Rukh to return into play. So, the first turn Dark Ritual-Hidden Horror-Rukh combo? Not legal. At least five of their wins leaned partly on the Rukh Egg foible. And last season's 12-game win streak is without question in question. Once the faulty card play was rectified, the jugger-nought became mortal. They did drop their first round series to #9-seeded Darwin. Season 6 will tell us what to really make of this team.

The Doom fall back a bit after their best season in team history. They were +200 in damage point differential in Season 4 and fell to only +17 this season. Their U-DPI dropped by a scary 70%. Six losses were due to mana issues, but others were close, so not a lost season at all, especially given their outstanding Cinderella playoff run in which they went 15-8 against seeds 10, 8, 1, 5 & 6. Many of their victories were due to defensive players (Tranquility, City of Solitude & Hurricane) and card advantage (Fastbond & Sylvan Library). Their finale against NLFY was down to the wire and arguably favored Darwin given the first four games. They dropped Game 5 by one measly point, and then Game 7 due to mana troubles. As wild a finish as we've had in the finale.

Victory!! Fans rejoice as their beloved No Landia take home a second crown (also won in S1)!! The doubters, the haters, the game-wagerers and the naysayers have all been silenced. Their regular season was anything but impressive as NLFY notched their lowest win percentage at an even .500 since Season 2. NLFY only dealt 14.75 damage points per game so their U-DPI (0.074) was less than average. That said, the DNA of this team is not to deal heavy blows, they're content with simply outlasting their opposition. In the playoffs, they swept up their long-time nemesis Smashed Potatoes in three games, then dismantled one-hit-wonder Murphy's Law in 4 games in the semi-final. The home team won every game in the finals series. NLFY and Darwin duked it out for the full seven games. Three games were decided by less than 5 points. A wild finish to the best season yet.

narrative.preview: Rookie squads - Big Gloves, The Anti-Dopes, Team 11 & the Glop Artists - look tough as nails. The Originals whittle down to three teams after one former great is forced to rebrand (Helloooo, Dark N Stormy). Big bruisers like Smashed Potatoes, Swamp Things and Bad Ass Fault are locked and re-loaded. Icy Hot is back due to popular demand (?). The much anticipated Season 6 is upon us. Bring the rain.

teams.writeup: Yeeeeeeeeeeeesh, that was a long season for the Hot. A 4-16 outing puts them at a 11-29 all-time record over their two seasons. A Kitty Cat Level of Ferocity with this team. They were the punching bag of the league, showing up to the battlefield with match-up apathy, just waiting for the KO. Surprisingly, their Team Captain, Manabarbs, was quite literally a game-changing card who we definitely haven't seen the last of. Penalizing the opponent for using mana was a fun new wrinkle for The League, and some teams were swallowed up by it. Put the fault on the Commish for not surrounding him with enough talent. A bevy of bounce cards looked decent on paper but cooked in play. Even a mid-season roster shake-up, thanks to noted fan Paul Kern, couldn't save them.

Ya know, this one hurts for several reasons. Teams have come and gone hoping to put old favorite Yawgmoth Demon into a winnable situation; Team 10, Frozen Stiffs. Pairing him with banjo artist Titania's Song seemed like a match made in heaven. A nucleus dependent on artifacts by...artifacts who could play D and go on offense? Hoo-rah! But the pairing was clunky, slow and put less pressure on opponents than envisioned. They had several good wins during the season, and a few painful losses, but the chemistry just isn't there. In the off-season, it's become clear that there's enormous potential for a predominantly mono-artifact outfit to be led by the banjo artist herself.

For the first time in League History, a Team was entirely replaced partway through the season. And in Season 6 this happened twice! The Anti-Dopes, built to mirror the opponent with copy spells, sat at 1-5. Most good teams have a way to put pressure on the opponent with offense and defense. This Team couldn't do either. Here's a telling comment from Week 6, 'Really have nothing going. They're not a good team, or even really a team. It's an assembly of cards that aim to thwart the opponent by prolonging the game and forcing a mirrored attack. Need an injection of new life here.' Instead, we put this Team to rest.

The Glop Artists, intending to pair Mask-Nought with a vigilance combo featuring Meekstone, was 2-5 at Week 8. The Glop Artists spent their first 7 games waiting around for the combo to hit. They were left with bloated hands and falling victim to their own sludgery with Winter Orb. The parts just didn't fit right. Pairing these two themes just didn't make sense, nor did it feel right to include Winter Orb and Meekstone with Mask-Nought anymore (Frozen Stiffs failed here, too). Alas, a major shake-up was in order for this team as well.

Mask-Nought needed more help getting one another in play, staying in play, and coming back into play if destroyed. Instead of pairing this duo with another strategic pillar, the Mist Monsters were built to focus almost entirely on supporting this combo, adding to the mystique of the Mask with low-cost creatures meant to confuse the opponent. The Monsters were up and down the rest of the way, finishing with a 6-8 record. They excelled in the Playoffs by blanking #10-Team 11 (2-0), then outlasting #7-Big Gloves (3-2) and pushing the #1-Potatoes to 5 games before losing. A strong finish. Next season will see the original rules followed and expectations are for a much faster, much stronger team.

With the Glop Artists stripped for parts, there was room for another entrant into the League. Red was added to the original green and white roster, as was a zoo of low-cost creatures to flank Lhurgoyf & Wrath of God. They went 9-4 the rest of the way, including a 7-game win streak to close out the season. This Team won 5 games with only 1 or 2 mana in play - that's insane! They rode that momentum into the playoffs, surprising Dark N Stormy in Round 1 (3-2) and then pushed #1-seeded Potatoes to 6 games before going down (4-2). One of those games against the Potatoes was record-setting, taking more turns than any game in history; 32-turns. Really one hell of a turnaround for a team that had previously gone 15-23 all-time. Welcome back from the dead, X.

A steady, reliable season for the Things who finished 8-12 in a stacked field. Their graveyard resurrection strategy continued to develop and they remained a tough out for any team that couldn't neutralize the Buried Alive + Living Death combo. A mid-season roster tweak added some speed to an otherwise methodical outfit. They were bounced in the first round of the playoffs but showed enough promise to suggest bigger things ahead in Season 7.

Oh yeah! Taking a flyer on this wacky, from left-field Team pays off as one more solid entrant into The League. Check out the commentary after their W2 loss to NLFY, 'This team is good. Still figuring out how to play them, but man are they capable of putting the heat on fast.' Yup, these hand-helpers are here to stay despite going 1-5 by Week 7. From that point on, the Gloves went 8-6 to take 7th place at 9-11. Underworld Dreams is one of the few true competitive pillars of this game, and pairing it with pieces like Teferi's Puzzle Box, Howling Mine and other draw cards was rhythmic and pulverizing. That said, their hasty pace was double-edged; they were #2 in damage given per turn, but dead last in damage taken per turn. A little more defense is needed to level them up.

Total enigma, this team. Their 9-11 record was good for 8th place this season. An average year in terms of record and offense despite an improvement over last season. Their wins were founded on the continued quick pace (4th in W-TPG; 7.67) and were buoyed by an even stronger emphasis on defense; Crumble and Winter Blast. In fact, Crumble alone helped win three games and more artifact-centric opponents are on the horizon. Green doesn't offer much in the way of staunch defensive cards, but that's where we'll start in the off-season. There's some danger in overreacting here - they were only one win away from taking home the championship last season.

A very strange season for the Fault, finishing in 6th place at 10-10 after playing by the ACTUAL rules for a change. They had a number of truly awesome comebacks worth special mention. Aside from that, though, the Fault showed some serious weaknesses on both sides of the battlefield. Offensively, their creature base was severely limited, and if Megrim didn't show, they sputtered. Defensively, they have a real tough time dealing with teams who can throw throngs of small creatures into battle.

The Smoke Makers! Alive and well and scorching faces off! An incredibly promising first official season for the direct damage heavers built on the heels of Fire Drilled's legacy had them finish in 4th place at 12-8, narrowly missing the #3 seed. Stormy was Top 3 in both U-DPI (0.115) and DPT (2.01). The Team doubled-down on low-cost flamethrowers, like Lightning Bolt, Incinerate and Chain Lightning, to keep competitive with a increasingly nimble league. Their aggressive style combined with low-cost spells had them finishing most games with almost half as many cards in-hand as opponents. To improve, the Smoke Makers could use a little more creature defense.

Back to their winning ways, the Slivers closed down the regular season at 14-6 to take 2nd place. Their 0.159 U-DPI was best in the league and good for 2nd All-Time. Their offense was #1 in the league (2.89 DPT) and good for #2 All-Time. The Slivers were ferocious this season and it felt like the championship was theirs to lose. They also had the 4th ranked defense, and the fastest win-pace in the league at 7.29 turns per win. They ran a balanced attack this year and once again benefited from the variety of mana producers on their team. On defense, nothing hurt the Slivers more than artifacts. Their post-season demise echoed a familiar refrain: 'What a disappointing finish. Seeing the Vise on the first turn was nearly doomsday - they aren't equipped to fend off the artifacts and were too slow out the gate to overcompensate.'

The defending champ had their best regular season in two years, nailing down 3rd place on a 13-7 record. Their U-DPI rose from 0.074 last season to 0.085, namely on a stronger defensive outing. No Landia held teams to only 1.18 DPT (2nd best). Further research showed that NFLY does a heck of a job destroying opposing lands. No Landia's playoff outing was solid, defending the crown well, beating fellow-land-crusher Bad Ass Fault in five (3-2), then handling the high-powered Slivers (4-2) before falling flat against the Potatoes (4-0). It was an odd finale. NLFY looked like they were completely out of gas and maybe they should have been after winning the 'ship in 7 last year.

Reclaim the crown with a demonstrative sweep against rival #3-NLFY in the finale. What a way to take it home! They rode nice momentum after dispatching #5-Extinction in the semi-finals (4-2), and outlasting the enigmatic #11-Mist Monsters in the opening round (3-2). Capturing the #1-seed with a 15-5 record was their first ever. How'd they do it? Defense. The average damage taken per turn across the league was 1.78. The Potatoes, by comparison, allowed only 0.77 damage per turn. That's good for 3rd All-Time. From a pace perspective, they took their time, using over 11 turns per game to reach an outcome. The key to their victories was not only land destruction (Armageddon, Cleansing, Balance) but the ability to benefit from it via Land Tax / Zuran Orb. Time for the Potatoes to sit back, simmer and smile at their two championships in 3 years.

The Anti-Dopes, built to mirror the opponent with copy spells, sat at 1-5 before being entirely replaced by the Mist Monsters. For the first time in League History, a Team was entirely replaced partway through the season. The Mist Monsters stepped in to give the Mask-Nought combo the focused support it always needed, finishing 6-8 the rest of the way and making a strong run in the playoffs.

The Glop Artists were 2-5 at Week 8 before being overhauled. Red was added to the original green and white roster as was a zoo of low-cost creatures. Extinction went 9-4 the rest of the way, including a 7-game win streak to close out the season. A stunning turnaround from a team that had been 15-23 all-time.

narrative.preview: We're back!! That's right! We're back baby!! Season 7 is upon us at last! Once again, The League is expanding - this time to 16 teams. Armed to the teeth with battlefield accoutrements, we see the much heralded return of several Original 10 Teams -- Thrull Frottle, Fire Drilled, Team 10 -- who have been revamped for combat. The Drillers play off the former mana surplus strategy while Team 10 puts 'Ol Yawggy and Winter Orb back together and the Thrulls, well, they remain unchanged..? Then we see a new entrant duo -- Clockwork & YCBI. Machine is the offspring of Team 11, building entirely around Titania's Song, defense and some absolute bruisers. YCBI is an honorary nod to Dad's original Zephyr Falcon Team with a few strategic pillars thrown in, like Kismet and Meekstone. The Mist Monsters shift again, pairing the Mask-Nought combo with Field of Dreams and a host of millers to play D. This season features four divisions of four teams each. It's gonna be bananas. Also, the crowned champion of Season 7 will be thrust into cardboard battle with Boss Fight in a best of 5 series with initial life totals increased to 30 each.

teams.writeup: Building a roster around Titania's Song has proven difficult, but I think I learned another key reason why after playing with them in the playoffs; a 4CMC centerpiece card is hard to summon. With so many teams building mana-defense strategies, it becomes increasingly important for any team to keep a low-CMC profile. Portcullis falls into the same trap. So, Peek-a-Boom ends up waiting around strategy-less. It's actually sort of maddening! They also don't pass the 'fun to play with' test, which is kind of important. It's no wonder Peak-a-Boom is destined for the recycling bin as we close the books on Season 7.

This is perhaps the most confounding team in the history of the League. They put up a paltry 5-15 regular season record then power right thru the Qualifier Bracket with wins over Peek-a-Boom (2-1) and Bad Ass Fault (3-0) before falling to one of the only teams that actually wants to constantly shuffle their library; Mist Monsters (4-1). Their 'boom or bust' style actually reminds me of Murphy's Law. When they draw well, they're nearly unstoppable. When they don't, they're a swift execution. The fun part, though, is how to maximize the value of their MVP; Underworld Dreams.

This one's easy; the Thrulls are 28-48 all-time after taking a 5-15 nosedive in Season 7. Putting them in 'The Crossroads' is borderline outrageous. Everything about this team suggests a teardown is imminent. Even the focus on Hecatomb is risky, frankly, and leaves them without much of a second move if/when it falters. Maybe it's not a centerpiece card after all. Lord of the Pit, on the other hand, is intended to be more of a fun gimmick yet actually had some run this season. What about diversifying the creature-base to move away from Thrulls-only to inject a little more versatility? One thing is for damn sure, they're absolutely terrible.

Here we are again, Drillers. You gave us a spirited run winning four straight (2-0 vs Thrulls) before running into the YCBI buzzsaw in Round 2. Any strength here should be given an asterisk as they seemed to excel against two teams that didn't focus on land destruction. YCBI turned out to be a perfect foil for them. Their ability to defend the Driller creatures (Meekstone + Castle), slow down mana supply (Kismet) and attack unopposed with flying creatures shifted the balance entirely in favor of YCBI by Game 3. One thing is for sure, this version of Fire Drilled - where they're all in on Mana Flare - isn't coming back. It's just too slow to compete.

So the summary is...DnS win nearly 50% of their games (9-11) yet lose a tight series in the first round against the #7-seeded NLFY. They struggled to find an identity, shuffling 30 cards in/out throughout the season. The secondary strategy beyond low-CMC smoke makers is still a question mark. Per game metrics vs. last season were confounding - more turns with more mana to summon many more cards and yet their damage per turn or per game are down considerably AND they're taking more heat. It's tempting to want to run this back with draw engines on the team, like Howling Mine, to ensure that the well of low-CMC direct damage spells never runs dry.

What has happened to the once mighty yet now faulty Fault? They fell out of the top-6 by W8 and staggered the rest of the way. This is somehow a defense-first team but they couldn't protect the rim against anyone, giving up a staggering 1.96 Damage per Turn. They shuffled out 25 players this season, a pretty high mark. The twist this time was to lean more heavily on M-Kill as a strategy even if it meant losing their own acreage. It didn't go too well, going 2-6 after W13. Megrim is such a devastating card, and the M-Kill strategy still somehow seems complementary. There are two angles I can see with this roster: swap out mana-kill cards for more hand-kill cards, or add even more mana-kill cards to further suffocate opponents.

It feels like an eternity ago that they won the Season 5 championship in 7-games against Darwin. The league has changed a lot since then. For one, nearly half the teams from that season have been dismantled. No Landia has made a few adaptations here and there, but they've gotten a bit slower by adding high-CMC cards like Demonic Hordes, Hellfire, Sengir Vampire and Nevinyrral's Disk. In this playoffs, it was clear they were too slow to really compete. Ironically, they don't really have a secondary strategy to complement their focus on land destruction. How can they get leaner, faster and more complementary to their core strategy?

I love this outfit and so want them to be better. Their regular season record and key offensive statistics are a bit misleading. They played in the weakest division - their three divisional contemporaries were each under .500 posting a combined 19-41 record in the regular season and a 1-8 playoff record. They did prove competitive outside of the division with some solid wins. They were one victory away from making noise in the second round of the premier bracket. YCBI made things difficult with their C-Freeze strategy and drew kindly in the final two games with both Archangel and Wrath of God. But Darwin is tough and the additions of Living Lands + Overrun are fantastic.

What a run! I have to eat my words this time having stated 'I'm not long on this team' in the Regular Season Recap. Oh man, did they prove me wrong, winning their way to the second round of the Premier Bracket. They went the distance against every opponent, beating a surging FD (3-2), then a spunky Darwin team (4-3) before going down to the #1-seeded Extinction (4-3). Their strategy was really effective, locking down opposing creatures with ease and taking their time to build an attack. Archangel was a massive free agent signing before the playoffs, winning Game MVP in 6 of 10 wins. He was an absolute bruiser to defend and most teams just couldn't do it. All in all, a pretty great season to build on.

A tale of two things! A solid regular season team quickly morphed into a ferocious playoff squad. The Things were very active on the free agent market just before their Round 3 match-up against Team 10. The artist formerly known as 'Big Gloves' was put out to pasture after going down to the Mist Monsters and the Things capitalized by ransacking the remains for several draw-advantage cards and a couple of bruisers (Crimson Hellkite, Thundermare). In their Round 3 series, they straight up haunted Team 10, winning 4 times in six tries by an average 17.75 points on only 6.25 turns per contest. They then ran into an absolute buzzsaw in Sliver Lining who won every game in five turns. This has never happened in the history of The League.

'They seem well-positioned to beat almost any type of team, except those featuring heavy creature defense.' This was one of the closing comments made in the Regular Season Recap. Who'd they play in the first round? A Swamp Things unit that had as many massive mutants as the irrepressible Boss Fight team. It was clear from the get-go that Team 10 would need a strong showing from Winter Orb to have a chance. It surfaced in one game, Game 5, which they won, but that was it. Spirit of the Night and its Protection from Black was tough to defend and the Things were frankly too fast. It's possible they need a bit more offensive firepower beside their rather lean line of fighters, or maybe they just need another go.

There's nothing quite like getting a little zany with team construction and seeing it work. The Machine had a heck of a season, going 11-3 after going whole hog into the mono-artifact scheme. Their regular season turnaround led to an incredibly arduous matchup against the Mist Monsters. In historic fashion, the Monsters took Game 1 in only three turns. They have an identity, though, and something fun to build on entering their first full season as an artifact only squad. They're chock full of robots who love to poke and prod. The unique thing is their combination of speed + heft. There's no limitations to what this group might throw onto the battlefield because of how much mana they can generate.

What a phenomenal season. Their second one as a team, but really the first when you consider how their dynamic duo - Illusionary Mask & Phyrexian Dreadnought - was played. Their playoff run was strong, with victories over the winner of the Qualifier Bracket #15 Big Gloves (4-1), then handled the surging #4 Clockwork (4-1) in the Quarterfinals thanks to Maze of Ith and their bruiser combo. In the Semi-finals they took a commanding 3-0 series lead against #1 Extinction before closing it out three games later. The Finals were a struggle, as they lost games due to 'too much mana' and 'idle play'. They rely heavily on Maze of Ith to neutralize opposing creatures, but a card like Crystalline Sliver completely neutralized it in the Finals.

Earlier exit than anticipated for big X. They fell to the Monsters in six games in the semifinals after starting down 3-0. This is a gritty team, no doubt, but they may have played down to the competition in the playoffs. After all, they were taken to seven games by massive underdog YCBI in the quarterfinals. In fact, Extinction played the lowest seed in both rounds and was beaten 7-6. Nevertheless, I find they are here to stay. Their roster is hell bent on outright destruction, and they're good at bringing the fiery rain, but maybe they could use some more offense. Nothing in the big CMC department, but just more tenacity upfront.

I just didn't think we'd get here, writing about the Smashed playoff run before a champ was crowned. The Potatoes were just so good this season, namely on the defensive end, but it was their offensive shortcomings that ruined their chances at a repeat. They amassed only 28-damage in their four game sweep against the Slivers in the semi-finals - that's 7 damage per game on average. Pitiful. The commentary from their losses echoed one thought; creature defense. They just couldn't contend with the super-fast attack of the Slivers. The League looks to be heading this way too, with perhaps 1-3 more Sliver copycats on the way. The Potatoes owned a 1-6 record against the Slivers this season.

Hell yeah. They did it!! Seven full seasons and they finally did it. In Season 1 they finished 2-8, and then 4-8 in Season 2. It's been a long, iterative road to win the crown. This team was real good in the playoffs, losing only 3x in wins against the revamped #8 Swamp Things (4-1) in the Quarterfinals, the defending champion Smashed Potatoes (4-0) in the Semifinals and then, of course, the bruising #5 Mist Monsters in the Finals. If you remove two outlier victories against the Potatoes, the Slivers averaged less than 6-turns per game (5.7) in playoff wins, including 6 wins in either 4 or 5 turns. That's crazy fast. In fact, in their series win against #8 Swamp Things, they averaged 5-turns per game. Really incredible. Another interesting playoff datapoint is the fact that this was yet another champion who finished under the increasingly relevant DTT champ line at a scant 1.19.

report_cards.mvp: Hecatomb OR Lord of the Pit
report_cards.writeup: This is an odd group, and I'm not quite sure what to make of them. On one hand, they started the season 1-11, ended up posting the 11th worst U-DPI in history and felt like the league's punching bag. On the other hand, they led the league, by far, in Deployed-W at only 4.80 meaning they worked less to get their Ws. Winning came one of two ways for this ugly bunch; weathering foes down with Hecatomb or a super-fast deployment of Lord of the Pit.

Have to say, I'm shocked about this one. Here I thought the Gloves could rebound, play some D and befuddle even the best teams with their constant hand shuffling. But no! Instead, the Big Gloves were historically bad. It didn't occur to me until a loss to the Slivers in W17 that the U-Dreams strategy can't drive but, rather, needs to ride shotgun.

report_cards.statmuse: 3-8 post-rebrand not counting Ws v #16 Thrull Frottle
report_cards.writeup: Icy Hot starts the season 0-6, pivots into Peak-a-Boom and finishes 6-8. Big success story until you look into those wins with half of them coming against the lowly Thrulls. Titania's Song feels like a tantalizing card with 'build around me' potential. But several teams have now built around it with little success.

The return of the Drillers was exciting yet met with an admission of guilt. The league has gotten faster every year and more teams are putting an emphasis on mana-defense specifically. FD is an all-firepower squad, at least in theory, who thrives in mana surplus. This season, they amassed the third lowest damage per turn in the league.

report_cards.statmuse: 13.13 Avg Turns per Win (highest this season)
report_cards.writeup: Wow! Dad's homage team squeaked out a solid first season! YCBI's style certainly lends itself to his personality - sit back, build defenses, wait for the right shot and then unleash the mighty birds for an air raid. The Meekstone - Pacifism - Castle trio work well together.

What has happened to the once mighty yet now faulty Fault? They fell out of the top-6 by W8 and staggered the rest of the way. The twist this time was to lean more heavily on M-Kill as a strategy even if it meant losing their own acreage.

report_cards.statmuse: 9.82; Only team bringing opponents to <10 life when they lose
report_cards.writeup: I'm just so disappointed. I love this team. Somehow this team transitioned from 'all-offense' to 'we'll try to play defense?' They're tough to beat, but the shift to defense seems to have come at the sacrifice of their offense.

report_cards.statmuse: 3; their mode ranking despite a 9th place finish
report_cards.writeup: What a strange season for Team 10. They start out 5-0, then lose to the only other undefeated team in Smashed Potatoes, get their doors blown off by the Slivers in W7, and march to a 9-3 record and 3rd place by W12.

The Things were in the toughest division. Having to compete multiple times with the #2 & #3 seeds, Smashed Potatoes and Sliver Lining respectively, and somehow emerge above .500 is a coup. This was actually their second best season ever by U-DPI and win percentage.

NLFY stamps their sixth winning season with an 11-9 record. Pretty impressive considering that six of their losses were due to a poor mana draw. Might even be a record. Their best win came against the Slivers in W9, deploying 11 cards over ten turns to gradually knock out the horde.

What an interesting season for Darwin. They start out 6-6 with a few solid wins against top teams. Their slight roster adjustment to include Living Lands, Overrun and Waiting in the Weeds paid dividends, contributing significantly to three wins.

How 'bout those Monsters!? In their first full season they turn and remove heads with a fast 9-3 start, leaning explicitly on the Mask-Nought combo to win games. In fact, not a single game was won without those two.

'Surprised' doesn't quite do it justice, but 'shocked' and 'enthralled' might! After their pathetic 1-5 start, the Machine replace 21 players on the roster, TWENTY-ONE! They kick the green scheme and Titania's Song to the curb for an all-Artifact regiment and it worked fantastically.

report_cards.statmuse: Top Defense; 1.04 Damage Against per Turn
report_cards.writeup: The Potatoes looked like they were gonna run away with our 7th season early on. They stampeded their way to 9-0 and held on firmly to 1st place from W5-W17. The top ranked defense this season says it all.
Only eight teams in League history had recorded a U-DPI of 0.130 or greater before this season. The feisty Slivers hold three of those spots, and added one more in Season 7 with a 0.139 U-DPI.

There you have it, the 4th most powerful team in league history (0.153 U-DPI) and #1 overall seed of Season 7 with a 15-5 record. What a ridiculous, shocking run this was, going 13-1 to finish the season.

narrative.preview: Three years!? It's been three years!?!? Well, there you have it folks.. Season 7 was such an overnight sensation (See: took 2 yrs) that yours truly needed >1,000 days to run it back! Fortunately, the medley of teams on a hella-low simmer for Season 8 are looking saucy as ever; the Slivers are back to defend their first title, NLFYs off-season was eerily quiet, Darwin's pissed off (again) and so are Extinction, Dark n Stormy and Team 10 - all of which remain largely unchanged from last season. Bad Ass Fault is sporting some new fancy H-Kill. YCBI found its groove in the playoffs and is prancing in bright eyed and bushy tailed. The Swamp Things are downright terrifying. Clockwork's nuts and bolts parade worked like clockwork in Season 7 so they're obviously back to cause problems. That makes 10 returners from the obscenely long Season 7 meaning six new entrants take the stage this time around.

teams.writeup: Sorry, Dad. YCBI helped remind me just how abhorrent it is to play a control deck (thanks, Stasis!) and how I'd like to avoid them at all costs if possible. Yeeesh, this was brutal! Most losses were logged as 'No contest,' which tracks, but the late-season improvement suggests a deck still searching for the right shape. The record is brutal. The foundation might not be. There's an elegance to the Vigilance ability and one that'll be explored in more detail in the off-season.

This one hurts.. an O-10 team, these guys just felt flat, stuck in no man's land without a real identity. The black and green hits so good, and I loved finding a way to enable 'Ol Yawggy, but it's just 'too hard' with this roster. Arguably their best angle to do damage was Black Vise - a copycat of NLFY which just felt bad. Ideally each team has unique win conditions and almost no card overlap whatsoever. The most enjoyment I got, outside of seeing Yawggy hit the field, was watching the artifact gameplay around Relic Barrier with Ankh / Orb to mess with opponents.

Another OG heads out to pasture, but hey it just never clicked. Ever since the Rukh Egg farce of Season 4 they've been on a seemingly wayward mission to find themselves. This season's Bottomless Pit addition showed some spunk but they had no obvious complement. Core to the strategy is discarding opponent cards, so maybe graveyard manipulation plays a role? There could be something to explore between Bad Ass Fault and Swamp Things, and explore we shall.

Finally, no more potatoes!! The goddamn poster child for insufferable game play, the Potatoes' 'Land Tax + Armageddon' combo will annoy us no more! Resetting the game with ever-generating tokens is tough sledding, and we reached a breaking point this season, dreaming instead of a mono-white outfit with spunk, style, identity and cohesion without the drawbacks. Alas, a new squad is forming to hit those notes.

Some teams in this league are just boom-or-bust. Clockwork was blanked in the playoffs, 3-0, by Folk Music. They played two well fought games, one at the hands of Energy Flux - literally the only card in the league designed to decimate the gears on this squad - and another because of Maze of Ith. You might say Folk Music was uniquely advantaged against Machine, a fair and accurate assessment given Energy Flux's multiple appearances in the series. This is a funky team, no question, and are still a few revisions away from being a tough out. Rocket Launcher was a fun addition. They'd benefit from some more defense, probably. For now, they're weird, nostalgic and at times pretty powerful. Good enough to take another lap in Season 9.

Gotta hand it to 'em, the Doomers hung in there with the Slivers in Round 1 (3-2), forcing a Game 5 after a gutsy 4 to -9 victory in Game 4. Darwin's season was a bit wonky donkey, cycling 45+ cards to try and find an identity. By seasons end, I think they did it. The Sylvan Library + Call of the Wild + Worldly Tutor + Big World Beaters is super fun and seems to have promise. In retrospect this makes a ton of sense - green employs some big boys, including an all-time favorite in Force of Nature, and I'm excited to see if there's enough of a draw engine around them to be viable and, dare I say it, competitive.

Underworld Dreams continues to vex ye ol Commish. Long established as a foundational card, singing loudest while opponents reload. Not a primary strategy, but maybe not a secondary one either? They wrapped up a 7-15 season, good enough for 15th overall and the #7 seed in the North Conference - a 'not great Bob' conclusion to the regular season, then proceed to stun the 2nd overall team, Mean Machine, by sweeping them in Round 1 (3-0). Round 2 was a different story yet perfectly encapsulated the Shuffler conundrum: Howling Mine really has to be a one-sided benefit. Perhaps U-Dreams can only work if it's (a) complementary, (b) partners with a lock strategy of some kind and (c) likely cannot exceed dual color deck design.

Win their Round 1 series against longtime foe, Team 10 (3-1) in a slight upset before getting humbled in Round 2 by TTFs (3-0). Swamp Things feel very 'boom-or-bust' to me with a game plan highly dependent on the Living Death combo. There are times where Unstable Mutation plays a sizable role, and I think that angle needs to be preserved. I also realized about mid-season that they support 'red' yet don't really use it - this after a few games of namely black/blue hands with too much red mana. It makes sense to test a sans-red version in Season 9. Outside of a sterling 15-5 affair in Season 5, they're 48-52 all-time.

Even Steven over here, the Stormy's are 32-30 across their 3 seasons in The League - but a consistent force in the playoffs. Underseeded against both opponents, Dark N Stormy took it to #4 Extinction in Round 1 (3-2) and #1 NLFY (2-3 in losing Round 2 effort). DnS was arguably the perfect foil for a board-clearing Extinction team with nary a single creature to lose in gameplay. Their speed helped too, taking two games in 5-turns. Against #1 NLFY, the Storm took a 2-1 lead after a 7-turn 3 to -4 down-to-the-wire victory in Game 3. DnS continues to win me over in the nostalgia department. The continuous flow of bolts and flames work wonders aesthetically and at times they are absolutely pulverizing.

'Plucky' is dead right. They ran into a tough opponent in Round 1, losing to Smashed Potatoes in a 3-game sweep. Only once in the series did they put real pressure on the Blancos. The Atts sac strategy ran into a buzzsaw as the Potatoes token brigade came on in full force with Kjeldoran Home Guard + Kjeldoran Outpost pumping out all manner of 1/1 soldiers and knights. Sacrificing a creature to destroy another creature is a compelling tactic - it's similar to what Extinction does, yet doesn't employ a Lhurgoyf equivalent. Hmm...

Lose a WILD Round 1 matchup against old foe; Dark N Stormy (3-2). Took Game 1 & Game 3, one of the few teams equipped to handle - and even benefit - from having their creature base lit on fire. Game 1 took 12-turns, Game 3 took 13. They lost Game 2 and Game 5 in 5-turns each, and Game 4 in 8-turns. Pace played a role in this series but so did the fact that X's main strategy - to wipe all creatures off the board - does next to nothing against DnS! One might say this was the very worst opponent for Extinction to play. One additional note; haste works really well for this team. Lightning Elemental played a larger role than anticipated this season.

A strong, surprising season by the mono-red outfit, but their first round exit to 7th seeded Hand Grenade tells a strange, slightly harrowing tale. Their Game 1 loss notes capture it well: 'Looked slow and clunky here - took awhile to get Gauntlet of Might out and then they couldn't use it (?). Goblin Recruiter feels like the only unlock for this team - I'd rather be sending goblins out every single turn for mass chaos.' The goblins, at least in my mind, should be a wild, reckless outfit with oodles of ammo and overwhelming swarm potential. This team ain't it yet. The Gauntlet of Might is probably not a good card in this deck. It's not yet clear how to better nail the WTF vibes while keeping them competitive, but that's a primary task for the off-season.

Unfortunately for everyone involved, this team is good and they further proved their mettle this post-season. The Music didn't lose a game until the semi-finals, sweeping #7 seeded Clockwork (3-0) in Round 1 then #6 seeded Smashed Potatoes in Round 2 (3-0) before going down in a respectable 7-games to #1 seeded TTFs. It was a series of swings, starting out 2-0 before taking three in a row, then finally dropping the last two. The Music were rhythmic, deafening and mesmerizing at once it seemed. They'll be back next season, surly and spiteful. A hell of a run, and so fun to have yet another very competitive, distinct team in The League.

The defending champ, the Slivers were one of the early favorites to contend for the Season 8 title. They survived a closer-than-expected series against Darwin (3-2) in Round 1 before blitzing Hand Grenade (3-0) in Round 2. The North Division semi-finals had them match up with #1 overall seed, No Lands For You, and that's when things turned south. NLFY ripped off four straight victories in an eye-opening sweep, decimating Colten's spirit in the process - Slivers #1 fan. The Slivers dropped the first two games in 6-turns each and namely due to Black Vise (Game MVP). They wrap Season 8 slightly humbled yet with a few tricks up their sleeves heading into Season 9.

Well, well, hello there #1 overall seed! NLFY pummels #8 YCBI (3-0) in the Round 1 opener before rallying back against #5 Dark N Stormy (3-2) after starting down 2-1. The semi-final sweep of #3 Sliver Lining was all too sweet, propelling NLFY to their fourth finals appearance. This year's finals were scintillating, with TTFs taking a 2-1 lead, then NLFY ripping off two in a row heading into Game 6. TTFs won handily by 21 pts in only 5-turns to force Game 7, and Game 7 came down to the wire. It was a crushing defeat for the all-blacks but they were right there!

It took a single season but in many ways, it's taken a lot longer than that. It took 8 builds to find the right shell for the illustrious Illusionary Mask - Phyrexian Dreadnought combo. Alas, it was worth the wait, as the Trick or Treat Freaks stampeded their way through Season 8 playoffs like a band of sugar-crazed 5th graders ding-dong-ditching a second neighborhood. The playoffs put them to the test, outlasting 8th seeded Bad Ass Fault in Round 1 (3-2), then pummeling Swamp Things in Round 2 (3-0) before taking it to their rival mono-blue outfit, Folk Music, in the Division Final (3-2). They saved their best for last with a 7-game thriller against the #1 overall seeded No Lands For You (4-3), including winning the final two games after being down 3-2. Three cards make this roster work; Scroll Rack, Transmute Artifact & Copy Artifact. They are the draw engine, and it's very unconventional - part of the reason it's so fun. Ironically, their predecessor, Mist Monsters, lost in the finals in Season 7 as a #5-seed. Thus, two seasons in a row the Mask-Nought combo has found postseason success. Congratulations Trick or Treat!

teams.team: Smashed Potatoes + The Circus → Smashed Circus
teams.writeup: Two teams merged mid-season to form Smashed Circus, combining their rosters and combining their records going forward. The merged outfit finished 9-13 overall, reflecting a season of adjustment as the two philosophies were integrated.

Overdrawn and Hand Grenade merged mid-season to form Over Grenade. The combined squad finished 7-15, as the pairing of Underworld Dreams with the artifact lock strategy showed flashes but couldn't find consistent footing.

report_cards.writeup: Sorry, Dad. YCBI helped remind me just how abhorrent it is to play a control deck and how I'd like to avoid them at all costs if possible. Most losses were logged as 'No contest,' which tracks, but the late-season improvement suggests a deck still searching for the right shape.

report_cards.statmuse: Went 2-9 in the 2nd half, worst mark in the league
report_cards.writeup: Bad Ass Fault lived on the edge all season. When the pieces aligned, the deck looked every bit as dangerous as advertised. Consistency was the missing ingredient. The Bottomless Pit + Megrim + The Rack combo is devastating, but how is it best supported?

Overdrawn's premise was simple — shuffle hands until you land the deadly combo and deal damage with Underworld Dreams. They stumbled to a 4-11 record prompting a total shift in construction back to Trick or Treat and Hand Grenade.

Clockwork entered their second season with grand aspirations, however stumbled out the gate losing their first 5 games. This roster requires excessive mana to function, so cards like Lotus Petal, Mana Vault and Urzatron are mission critical.

Strangest season yet for Darwin, yet an important one. They finished the regular season third worst in Damage Dealt per Game. The middling-ness prompted much card shuffling, giving way to a fork in the road for the mono-green OG: build around brute force or build around low-cost creatures.

report_cards.theme: Circus was so bad we had to bring back our least favorite team ever
report_cards.writeup: The Circus was arguably ill-conceived from the start. It's honestly a shock that they lasted 9 games. We did, however, remove the Zuran Orb from the deck, as it was the only way we could stomach play with the mono-white outfit once again.

report_cards.statmuse: Owners of a 5-game win streak + 4-game lose streak!
report_cards.writeup: Swamp Things were the league's ultimate boom-or-bust team. The highs came fast, while the lows arrived just as quickly. They were right around the league average on DGT, yet second-to-last in DTT, leaving little margin when things went sideways.

Team 10 is a fascinating squad, controlling more games than their record suggests — finishing them was the hard part. Not known for intense speed, the team led the league in Deploy-W. The bright spot was tempo control — few teams dictated pace as well.

report_cards.theme: Easy BateMobile, not too fast baby
report_cards.statmuse: Owner of the fastest win (3-turns vs Things) and the slowest (23-turns vs Sliver Lining)
report_cards.writeup: Ah, Dark N Stormy. You bring the nostalgia in droves, old friend! Dark N Stormy's record states the obvious — they either win or they lose and do one or the other roughly 50% of the time.

Extinction really found themselves this season, leaning heavily into Survival of the Fittest to further rally around Lhurgoyf / creature kill strategy. It's important to mention that two of their win streak victories came against Top 3 teams.

Out of the ashes of Thrull Frottle comes the promise of new mainstay; The Attritionists. This revised version, building around Grave Pact, supercharged the sac engine to great effect and fell back to the all-too-familiar might mites strategy with Bad Moon.

MJ was truly onto something! His Merfolk reincarnate deck fared real well, too well honestly, in their inaugural season. Folk Music defaults to mighty mites, buttressed by some of the best defense around and some chicanery.

At long last, the illustrious Mask-Nought combo has a home! Trick or Treat plays on the prior Mist Monsters configuration, surrounding the game-ending combo with tutors, draw advantage and counter defense.

report_cards.theme: The Pulis Avenue rims of The League
report_cards.statmuse: 3.22 DGT; only team 3+ in this category
report_cards.writeup: Man, where to begin. The Slivers, the defending champs, felt like the team to beat all season long. There wasn't a single game where they felt like the underdog. They were fast to win, resilient when they lost, high-impact on each turn and minimalist in deployment.

report_cards.theme: Bazinga! Goblins are here to stay!
report_cards.writeup: Mean Machine!! The all-reds spent the season doing exactly what they said they would do — and daring opponents to stop it. The identity was never clearer than Goblin Recruiter, who won six Game MVPs.
No Landia, you dog, you!!! The defending stalwart claimed their first ever #1 overall seed behind a stout 1.10 Damage Taken per Turn — comfortably the league's best defensive mark of the season.
